coredata.dc:title: Antibacterial carbon dots/iron oxychloride nanoplatform for chemodynamic and photothermal therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Xiang
Yang, Jie
Wu, Juan
Su, Han
Sun, Guowen
Ni, Yanhong
Sun, Weibin
coredata.dc:description: The inflammatory outbreak caused by abundant bacteria propagation in the infected wound area pose a considerable challenge to human health. To overcome this situation, here the therapeutic method is based on inspired chemodynamic therapy (CDT) induced by a novel iron oxychloride nanosheets (FeOCl NSs) catalyst with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and photothermal therapy (PTT) mediated by coating inorganic carbon dots (CDs) and organic polyethylene glycol (PEG) with near-infrared (NIR) emission. The redox cycle of ions on FeOCl NSs enables the generation of facile hydroxyl radical (·OH) from H2O2 activation for further CDT and induces bacteria death. CDs have been set as promising in vivo therapeutic agents due to their excellent biosafety and high photostability. In this study, the nanocomposites FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs are synthesized by coating the PEG and CDs on the FeOCl NSs, exhibiting a potent PTT responsive effect with 808 nm NIR laser, which highly enhances the antibacterial effect in the infected wound area.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemodynamic therapy
Photothermal therapy
FeOCl@PEG@CDs nanocomposites
Carbon dots
Antibacterial effect

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the typical synthesis process of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs. (b) TEM and HR-TEM images of FeOCl NSs. (c) XRD and (d) XPS analysis of FeOCl NSs. (e) TEM image of CDs. (f) TEM and HR-TEM images of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs. (g) Element mapping of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs.

(a) The ·OH generation capability of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs suspension was confirmed by DMPO trapped EPR spectra. (b) Confocal images of bacteria were detected with DCFH-DA probe incubation under various treatments (scale bar = 5 μm). (c) Photothermal performance of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs at various concentrations was detected under 808 nm laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2). (d) Photothermal profiles of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs solution with five irradiation cycles under 808 nm laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2). (e) Calculation of changes in FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs solution with five irradiation cycles. n = 3, each point represents a mean ± S.D. (f) Thermal infrared images of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs solutions (1 mg/mL) were recorded under 808 nm laser irradiation (1.5 W/cm2).

TEM images of (a) S. aureus and (b) E. coli after different treatments (scale bar = 0.5 μm). Viability quantitative results of (c) S. aureus and (d) E. coli after different treatments. Each point represents a mean ± S.D., p values: ***p < 0.001. (e) Infrared images of wound positions and (f) the corresponding temperature curves.

(a) Illustrative scheme of modeling and treating of an infected wound. (b) Representative photographs and (c) H&E staining images of the infectious site of mice after various treatments for 14 days (scale bar = 100 μm). (d) Changes in mouse body weights after various treatments. n = 5, each point represents a mean ± S.D.

head.ce:title: Antibacterial carbon dots/iron oxychloride nanoplatform for chemodynamic and photothermal therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Periodontology, Nanjing Stomatological Hospital, Medical School of Nanjing University, Nanjing, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Periodontology, Nanjing Stomatological Hospital, Medical School of Nanjing University, Nanjing, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Stomatology, Jinling Hospital, Nanjing, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Stomatology, Jinling Hospital, Nanjing, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The inflammatory outbreak caused by abundant bacteria propagation in the infected wound area pose a considerable challenge to human health. To overcome this situation, here the therapeutic method is based on inspired chemodynamic therapy (CDT) induced by a novel iron oxychloride nanosheets (FeOCl NSs) catalyst with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and photothermal therapy (PTT) mediated by coating inorganic carbon dots (CDs) and organic polyethylene glycol (PEG) with near-infrared (NIR) emission. The redox cycle of ions on FeOCl NSs enables the generation of facile hydroxyl radical (·OH) from H2O2 activation for further CDT and induces bacteria death. CDs have been set as promising in vivo therapeutic agents due to their excellent biosafety and high photostability. In this study, the nanocomposites FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs are synthesized by coating the PEG and CDs on the FeOCl NSs, exhibiting a potent PTT responsive effect with 808 nm NIR laser, which highly enhances the antibacterial effect in the infected wound area.

The FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs were prepared for precise PTT and CDT synergistic therapy of bacterial infection during the wound healing process. The developed FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs integrates the merits of the improved selectivity of Fe(II) toward •OH generation rather than conversion to Fe(IV) and specific heating directly to bacteria.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Wound infection induced by pathogens has been an enormous threat to human health for a long time [1–3]. Bacterial infection leads the primary cause and has drawn more attention in various fields. Nowadays, many drugs for bacteria elimination have been used, such as antibiotics and other chemical bactericides [4–7]. However, their long-term medication or abuse caused growing drug-resistant bacteria and even generating super bacteria [8–10]. Therefore, it is time to develop new and effective antibacterial strategies that are noninvasive and specific.

Recently, the inspiring advances in nanotechnology promise combating bacteria [11–13], such as chemodynamic therapy (CDT) [14–16] and photothermal therapy (PTT) [17–19]. For instance, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) can be turned into the hydroxyl radical (·OH) form through the Fe(II)/Fe (III) redox cycle induced Fenton reaction in the CDT procedure [20–23]. The generated oxidative ·OH, known as one type of the reactive oxygen species (ROS), would contribute to a more potent antibacterial effect by leading to bacteria damage [24–26]. Some CDT reagents with potent catalytic character have been applied in the infected wound treatment in previous studies [16,27]. However, the low catalytic activity becomes the limiting factor for CDT therapeutic effect in vivo, which needs to be improved in the following therapeutic system. Meanwhile, benefiting from the excellent absorption with near-infrared (NIR) laser [28–30], carbon dots (CDs) exhibit competitive NIR laser-induced photothermal effect [31,32], which supports directly killing bacteria through hyperthermia from PTT [33–36]. Therefore, developing a CDT/PTT reagent with bacteria elimination has enormous prospects in biomedical application.

The Fenton reaction relies on the Fe(II)/Fe(III) redox cycle for H2O2 activation. Due to its rich history, engineering simplicity, and low cost, the Fenton reaction has not been as widely adopted in practice. The activation processes of Fenton reaction employ laser and ozone. However, this resulted in a large amount of iron precipitation after neutralization. Heterogeneous catalysts such as iron oxychloride (FeOCl) have been explored to avoid iron precipitates [37]. FeOCl possesses an orthorhombic system with a layered structure, in which the adjacent layers are bonded across interlayer atoms by van der Waals interactions [38]. FeOCl is often used as the host material of intercalation compounds because of the weak interaction between the layers [39]. So far, only FeOCl plates have been synthesized by solid-phase or vapor-deposition synthesis methods [40]; as far as we know, the preparation of ultrathin FeOCl nanosheets (NSs) is a more convenient liquid-exfoliation method rarely found.

The theranostic reagent FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs is based on the FeOCl NSs catalysts and coated polyethylene glycol (PEG) and CDs. It was explored for double CDT/PTT antibacterial therapy in the near-infrared (NIR) region. As a suitable hydrophilic polymer, the polyethylene glycol (PEG) modified with thiol is used to construct the hybrid organic/inorganic coatings. H2O2 was applied in the infected wound area to provide substrate for Fe-induced Fenton reaction, which could inspire the CDT process by efficaciously generating the ·OH [41–43]. During this process, CDs also guaranteed the excellent PTT effect with 808 nm laser, which leads to local hyperthermia for bacteria death. The synthesis, multifunctional natures, and antibacterial performance of the nanocomposites FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs were tested in vitro and in vivo.

Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), FeCl3·6H2O, and methanol were purchased from Aladdin Reagents (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from Shengxing Life Technologies Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Polyethylene glycol (PEG, MW = 3000–15,000), were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without any purification. Deionized water (Millipore Milli-Q grade, 18.2 MΩ) was used in all experiments.

The morphologies were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL, JEM-2100F, 200 kV). UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra were recorded on a Lambda25 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) data were obtained with an E-580 (Germany). In vitro bright field and fluorescence images were collected with a confocal laser scanning microscope (TI-E-A1R, Nikon, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra was obtained with Phillips, Holland PW 1710 X-ray diffractometer system. Size distribution and zeta potential of the sample was obtained by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Nicomp™ 380 ZLS). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested by an Escalab 250Xi spectrometer. The experimental detail and characterization methods are in the Supplementary Information.

The FeOCl NSs were prepared by heating FeCl3·6H2O powder at 200 °C for 2.5 h (Fig. 1a). Then, the synthesized nanostructure was further characterized by kinds of modern scientific technics. Moreover, the FeOCl NSs are free-standing with a lateral size of about 200 nm (Fig. 1b). Moreover, the shape of crystalline FeOCl NSs was also exhibited using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image that clearly showed their distinctive lattice fringes with 0.343 and 0.309 nm (Fig. 1b). As shown in Fig. 1c, we obtained completely matching results with {010} and {110} facets. Besides, the specific peaks at 10.7, 26.1°, 35.5°, and 38.2° upon X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis were found responding to the {010}, {110}, {021}, and {111} crystal facets, respectively, which also kept pace with pure FeOCl crystals (JCPDS No. 72–0619) (Fig. 1c). The component elements of the FeOCl NSs were featured as O, Cl, and Fe via XPS analysis (Fig. 1d), which also revealed the trivalent state of the majority of Fe in the FeOCl NSs. The Fe 2p spectrum exhibited major peaks at 711.9 and 725.5 eV, ascribed to the generation of binding energies of 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 in Fe(III), respectively, along with as the satellite peaks at 718.6 and 730.1 eV. The separate Fe 2p3/2 peak detected at 714.1 eV suggested the existence of Fe(II) (Fig. S1). In addition, the prominent O 1 s peak (532.5 eV) (Fig. S2) and Cl 2p peak (199.2 eV) (Fig. S3) indicated the formation of FeO and FeCl bonds. DLS was utilized to monitor the hydrodynamic size of FeOCl NSs. The FeOCl NSs showed a uniform size of about 420 nm, signifying that the FeOCl NSs are suitable for biological applications (Fig. S4).

Typically, ·OH, as one of the types of ROS, was identified as the agent with high chemical activity and short life history, which contributed to broad applications in anti-bacteria [44]. Here, we detected the in situ generated amount of ·OH using EPR assay in Fig. 2a. Consequently, we found that the highest EPR value appeared only within multiple circumstances consisting of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus H2O2. Then, dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was applied to determine the ROS production with green fluorescence of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs through incubating bacteria with various media. In addition, methylene blue (MB) is a kind of dye that can be degraded by ·OH, and the UV–vis absorption intensity of MB decreases with the degradation of MB. As clearly shown in Fig. S7, the final removal efficiency gradually is 18.5% in FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs + H2O2 group. As shown in Fig. 2b, negligible fluorescence signal could be detected among the control, H2O2, and the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs group, indicating that free FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs could not lead to the generation of ROS. However, the green fluorescence signal became stronger with the existence of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus H2O2, which also supported that FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs could induce the strongest generation of ·OH in bacteria with the presence of H2O2.

Subsequently, FeOCl@PEG NSs were obtained by loading PEG upon FeOCl NSs by sequential electrostatic adhesion. The successful coating of PEG on the FeOCl NSs surface was identified by the difference that happened on the ζ potential that was changed from 11.2 mV to 8.6 mV. Next, to add the PTT characteristic to NCs, CDs were introduced to the FeOCl@PEG NSs surface (Fig. 1a). As presented in the TEM image (Fig. 1e), the CDs hold a highly uniform sphere-like structure with a size of about 5 nm. The interplanar distance of 0.23 nm was estimated in Fig. 1e, which was consistent with the (100) crystal plane of CDs. Furthermore, the small CDs and PEG polymer layer upon FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs can be observed on the surface of FeOCl NSs via TEM images, which exhibited the uniform distribution of the CDs (Fig. 1f). Furthermore, this coating method in our work had been shown by STEM-EDS with reasonable and excellent co-localization among distinct elements (Fe and Cl elements were from the surface coating FeOCl NSs when C element was from CDs and PEG) (Fig. 1g). To evaluate the stability of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs, in subsequent experiments, FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs were dissolved in PBS, FBS, Blood, and DMEM stored for 14 days. All solutions were clear, and no difference from the initial state was observed (Fig. S5). Zeta potential measurements are further used to confirm the stability of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs solution (Fig. S6). The zeta potential of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs is −15 mV, ensuring a more stable presence in blood.

Then, the photothermal properties of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs were studied in vitro. By recording the temperature change curve of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs solution under the irradiation of an 808 nm laser, we noticed that the curves were in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 2c). In addition, the temperature of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL presented an apparent increase up to 50 °C; in contrast, the temperature of water nearly remained the same. Moreover, the photothermal stability of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs solution was further evaluated. The temperature of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs solution was firstly increased to a steady temperature by irradiating with 808 nm laser and then recovered to room temperature. As a result, the temperature elevation had a slight and non-significant difference after three cycles of laser off/on, suggesting the high photothermal stability of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs solution (Fig. 2d-f). Furthermore, the designed FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs also exhibit fair photothermal efficiency (28.9%), making them ideal materials for wound heal.

To further explore the morphological change of the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs, TEM characterization was performed. As shown in Fig. 3a,b, under natural conditions, there was no morphological difference compared with the control group of PBS. However, due to the CDT and PTT efficiency, the bacterial surface became wrinkled in the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus H2O2 and FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus laser groups, respectively. It was noted that bacteria exhibited significant deformation with the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus H2O2/laser group. These results indicated that the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs exhibited an excellent PTT/CDT synergistic damage effect on bacteria. Furthermore, the antibacterial capability of the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs was further proved by bacterial survival experiments, which showed that the survival of bacteria exhibited different situations under various treatments. As expected, most bacteria were kept alive in the PBS and H2O2 treatment groups, while the treatments of FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus H2O2 or FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus laser group induced partly bacteria damage that would be ascribed to the generated CDT or PTT effect. Also, the most severe damage situation of bacteria occurred with the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus H2O2/laser treatment, indicating the excellent antibacterial property of PTT/CDT synergetic therapy (Fig. 3c,d).

The bacteria-infected mice were randomly divided into six groups and treated with various treatments (PBS, H2O2, FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs, FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus H2O2, FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus laser, and FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus H2O2/laser) to evaluate the in vivo PTT/CDT synergetic therapeutic effect followed the exhibited operation route (Fig. 4a). Fig. 3e,f shows the thermal images of the mice treated with PBS, only laser, and FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus laser, respectively. We found that the temperature in the wound area in the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus laser group rapidly increased up to 50 °C within 10 min under 808 nm irradiation. Moreover, by recording the recovery situation around the wound area, we found an evident scab on the skin of the mice treated with PBS, H2O2, or FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs after 14 days (Fig. 4b). However, this weak wound healing situation was rescued by the exogenous addition of H2O2 or 808 nm laser, which enhanced therapeutic effects in FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus H2O2 or FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus laser group. Furthermore, the best healing effect with no significant scab was observed in the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus H2O2/laser group owing to the excellent PTT effect of the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs. Finally, mice with various treatments were sacrificed to perform hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) histological analysis, which indicated a more intact skin tissue structure with a entire hair follicle and glands regeneration (red arrows) in the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus H2O2/laser treatment group after 14 days (Fig. 4c).

Next, being a suitable therapeutic agent for the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs, their biocompatibility property also needed to be explored. Herein, we recorded the mice weights among all groups after 14 days, which slightly floated among various treatments in the normal physiological range (Fig. 4d). After treatment, main organs from FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus laser group were collected and performed with H&E analysis. In contrast to the PBS group, no noticeable pathological tissue damages or abnormalities were observed in the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs plus laser group (Fig. S8). The consistent result was also reflected in the cell viability and dual-staining analysis in Fig. S9,10. The 4 T1 cells maintained the live state under various treatments as the captured green fluorescence from calcein-AM (Fig. S9). Furthermore, cell viability assay verified that the cell viability remained by 89% when the concentration reached 1.5 mg/mL (Fig. S10). Above all, we proved the excellent biocompatibility and biosafety of the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs as a therapeutic agent for the wound healing process.

In summary, we have reported FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs for precise PTT and CDT synergistic bacterial infection therapy during the wound healing process. The developed FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs integrates the merits of the Fe(II)’s improved selectivity toward ·OH generation rather than conversion to Fe(IV) and specific heating directly to bacteria. The good in vitro and in vivo antibacterial performances of the FeOCl@PEG@CDs NCs attribute to the combination of the CDT effect of FeOCl and the PTT effect of CDs. Considering the differences in the microenvironment between bacteria-infected regions and normal tissues, we believe that the activated PTT and CDT synergistic therapy enables the further development of more intelligent platforms for targeted bacteria-killing and enhanced wound healing effects.
All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript (e.g., technical help, writing and editing assistance, general support).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51772144).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colcom.2021.100552.
